artifacts.content:
From: Rachel Lindstrom <rlindstrom@stoughtonpubliclibrary.org>
To: Dana Morales <dmorales@stoughtongives.org>
Date: 2025-02-11 09:42
Subject: Follow-up Details for Youth Garden Micro-Grant Application

Hi Dana,

Thank you again for taking the time to talk through the Stoughton Community Youth Garden proposal yesterday. I really appreciate how concrete your feedback was about clarifying the “community safety” and “youth engagement” outcomes.

To your question about safety benefits: from a community safety standpoint, one unexpected benefit has been that two brothers I know through Eric’s 10th‑grade U.S. history class at Stoughton High—whose dad was arrested for a DUI in May 2023 on County Highway B and who started acting out at school afterward—now come to the garden on Tuesdays and Thursdays instead of roaming around the parking lot by the Stoughton Cinema 4. Eric and the vice principal had been documenting a string of minor vandalism incidents there last fall, and those have basically stopped since the boys started helping with watering and harvesting.

Regarding the budget section: I want to be transparent that I haven’t fully priced out all of the materials yet, so the amount I’m requesting is more of an educated guess based on what we spent on a different library-run outdoor program last year. I’m happy to tighten those numbers as I get updated quotes from the hardware store and print shop.

If you need to list a main program contact for your records, please use:

Stoughton Public Library – Youth Services
Attn: Rachel Marie Lindstrom
Phone: (608) 555-2743
Email: rlindstrom@stoughtonpubliclibrary.org

Feel free to call or email if anything in the narrative or budget needs clarification. I’ll aim to send the revised draft by Friday afternoon.

Best,
Rachel

Rachel Marie Lindstrom
Youth Services Librarian & Community Program Coordinator
Stoughton Public Library
214 South Forrest Street, Stoughton, WI 53589
(608) 555-2743

metadata.subject: Follow-up Details for Youth Garden Micro-Grant Application

artifacts.content:
Subject: Quick question about garden program contact info

Hi Rachel,

I’m finalizing the Stoughton Community Youth Garden blurb for the Parks & Rec summer booklet and just need to confirm what phone number you want listed as the main contact for the organization.

Last year, when we first talked about piloting the program, I wrote down the Stoughton Public Library youth services desk line as **(608) 873-3379**. I also have an older note from when you and I met about the after‑school reading buddies project that lists **(608) 873-4421** as “Rachel – work.” Before I send this to layout, can you clarify which number you actually want folks to call for garden questions and registration?

It might make sense to stick with a library number instead of your personal cell, especially since you’ll already be there most afternoons. That way, parents who call about the garden but have general library questions (or want to ask about your youth gardening book collection that was damaged in your basement flood at 214 South Forrest Street back in August 2023) can be transferred easily. You mentioned you were using some RoundUP funds to replace those books, so a library line seems logical.

If you can reply by Thursday at noon, I can still get this into the print run. Otherwise it’ll have to wait until the online update in June.

Thanks!

Megan Larson
Stoughton Parks & Recreation Department
(608) 873-9508

metadata.subject: Quick question about garden program contact info

artifacts.content:
Subject: RoundUP application – need a phone number for your organization

Hi Rachel,

I’m reviewing your RoundUP grant draft and it looks very strong. I just need to plug in a public contact phone number for the “organization information” section before we can move it to the next step.

Right now I see two different numbers in your past applications: one lists the Stoughton Public Library main line as **(608) 873-6281**, and another lists what I think is your old direct desk number, **(608) 873-3390**. Since you’re framing the Stoughton Community Youth Garden as a library‑affiliated program, I assume we should use a library phone rather than anything personal.

Also, your explanation of how funds will be used was very compelling—especially the part about setting aside about $200 so Paul can be reimbursed for mileage and occasional meals when he drives your mom, Carolyn, from Janesville to the garden on Wednesdays next summer, since she stopped driving after her mild cognitive impairment diagnosis at Mercyhealth Hospital in March 2022 but still wants to help kids with basic first‑aid and snack prep. That kind of detail really illustrates the community care aspect.

Send me a quick line confirming which number you prefer for the “organization information > phone” field and I’ll lock it in.

Best,

Dana Schultz
Program Officer, Dane County Community Giving Fund
(608) 555-9801

metadata.subject: RoundUP application – need a phone number for your organization

artifacts.content:
Draft RoundUP application – finalize org phone & budget

Block out time to polish the RoundUP request before Dana’s soft deadline.

To do:
- Decide which phone number to use for “organization information > phone.” Options in previous drafts: library main line **(608) 873-6281**, youth services desk **(608) 873-3379**, or the generic City of Stoughton switchboard **(608) 873-6677**. Do NOT list personal cell.
- Double‑check that the garden’s affiliation with Stoughton Public Library is clear so callers aren’t confused when they hear the library greeting.
- Re‑read the sections on how funds will be used, especially the part about replacing and expanding the youth gardening book collection that was ruined when the basement at 214 South Forrest Street flooded during the August 2023 storms, along with those IRS payment plan papers from when you and Eric fell behind on 2021 federal taxes.
- Tighten the community benefits paragraph about coordinating with Ms. Jensen so 10–15 pounds of harvest go to the Stoughton Food Pantry’s fresh produce section for families who also use the free Wednesday meal at Covenant Lutheran.
- Be honest but concise about capacity: if this RoundUP request doesn’t come through, you’ll probably have to scale the idea way back or even put it on hold, because you don’t have the bandwidth to add more fundraising on top of your regular job and family responsibilities.

Bring thermos of tea and noise‑canceling headphones.

metadata.title: Draft RoundUP application – finalize org phone & budget

artifacts.content:
Subject: Confirmation needed – phone number for pantry flyer

Hi Rachel,

The Stoughton Food Pantry is putting together a simple half‑sheet flyer listing local partners that regularly contribute fresh produce. We’d love to highlight the Stoughton Community Youth Garden, especially since you’ve been coordinating with Ms. Jensen to send 10–15 pounds of kid‑friendly veggies each week for the families who also come to the free Wednesday meal at Covenant Lutheran.

They’ve asked for a short description plus a phone number for people who want to learn more or sign up their kids. Right now I’ve tentatively listed the library’s general information line as **(608) 873-6281** with the note “ask for Youth Services.” One of the pantry volunteers thought your organization might prefer a more direct line and suggested we use **(608) 873-3379**, which I believe is the children’s room desk.

Can you let me know which number you want associated with the garden on this flyer? Since the pantry will print 300 copies, we want to avoid listing the wrong contact.

Also, the detail in your grant draft about setting aside around $200 so Paul can cover mileage and meals when he brings your mom from Janesville to help with first‑aid and snack prep really moved our board. It shows how thoughtfully you’re building intergenerational connections.

Please reply by Monday if possible.

Warmly,

Sister Elaine
Stoughton Food Pantry
(608) 873-7782

metadata.subject: Confirmation needed – phone number for pantry flyer

artifacts.content:
Meeting with Eric – decide on official garden contact phone

Agenda:
- Review options for what phone number to list as the “organization information > phone” on all 2025 materials (RoundUP application, pantry flyer, Parks & Rec booklet, website blurb).
- Possibilities:
  • Library main line: **(608) 873-6281** (pros: stable, always staffed; cons: callers have to navigate menu).
  • Youth services desk: **(608) 873-3379** (pros: more direct to Rachel; cons: sometimes unstaffed during programs).
  • City of Stoughton switchboard: **(608) 873-6677** (pros: neutral “city” number; cons: less personal).
- Confirm that we are NOT using Rachel’s personal cell on anything public.
- Talk through remaining edits on the RoundUP budget, including the small $200 set‑aside so Paul can be reimbursed for mileage and occasional meals when he drives Carolyn from Janesville after her mild cognitive impairment diagnosis, and the line item for replacing youth gardening books ruined in the August 2023 basement flood at 214 South Forrest Street.
- Reality check: if the RoundUP grant falls through, discuss how much we’d need to scale back or delay expansion rather than adding more fundraising to an already full plate.

Bring: laptop, latest grant draft, PTA notes about pantry partnerships.

metadata.title: Meeting with Eric – decide on official garden contact phone
metadata.location: Dining room table – 214 S Forrest St

artifacts.content:
From: Rachel Lindstrom <rachel.lindstrom@stoughtonlibrary.org>
To: info@stoughtonyouthalliance.org
Date: 2025-01-22 09:14
Subject: Contact info for youth garden application form

Hi Mariah,

I’m filling out the organizational information section of the RoundUP mini‑grant application and wanted to double‑check what phone number you’d prefer I list for the “organization phone” line.

Right now, on our flyers for the Stoughton Community Youth Garden we’ve been using the main library youth services desk line: (608) 873‑6284. Parents are used to calling that number to ask about story time, Wednesday evening homework club, or garden nights, and the front desk staff know to transfer youth garden questions to me.

I could also put my direct office line, (608) 873‑6291, but I’m a little worried about missing calls when I’m out at the garden site or in the middle of a program. We definitely do *not* want to list my personal cell again after that one Facebook post last fall—my phone was buzzing nonstop with questions about whether the garden would be open on Labor Day.

One of the clearest benefits to the Stoughton community, which I’m trying to capture in the narrative section, is that several of the middle schoolers from our Wednesday evening homework club—especially two 7th graders referred by the school social worker in March 2023 for anxiety and truancy—now have a safe, structured place to be after school in the garden instead of hanging out unsupervised behind the Kwik Trip on East Main. Their parents and I have all been really worried about that since last fall.

I know this won’t directly help with bigger issues Stoughton is facing, like housing or transportation; it’s more of a “nice‑to‑have” enrichment thing than something essential, but it still feels important.

Let me know which phone number you think is best for the form.

Thanks,
Rachel

metadata.subject: Contact info for youth garden application form

artifacts.content:
Title: Call with RoundUP program officer
Date: 2025-01-27 14:00–14:45
Location: Rachel’s office, Stoughton Public Library
Attendees: Rachel Lindstrom; Dana Schultz (RoundUP)

Description:

Agenda for call about Stoughton Community Youth Garden funding application.

1. Confirm organizational contact details
   - Legal name: Stoughton Public Library (Youth Services)
   - Mailing address: 304 S. Fourth St., Stoughton, WI 53589
   - Primary phone to list on application: use the library’s main switchboard, (608) 873‑8541, not Rachel’s personal cell and not the homework club line. Dana said they prefer a number that will still work if staff change.

2. Discuss how funds will be used
   - Explain that some of the RoundUP funds would quietly cover adaptive tools and extra staff time so that three students from Eric’s special education social studies class at Stoughton High School—including a 10th grader with a documented history of self‑harm and anxiety who was hospitalized at UW Health in April 2023—can safely participate in the after‑school garden sessions behind the library starting May 2025.

3. Clarify evaluation metrics
   - Attendance tracking for grades 3–10
   - Parent feedback surveys
   - Simple pre/post reflection prompts for kids

4. Timeline
   - Garden prep (soil, raised beds, ordering youth‑sized gloves and first‑aid kit) in April
   - After‑school sessions 2x/week May–September

Note: Have grant narrative and draft budget spreadsheet open during call. Remember to ask whether they need a secondary phone contact (e.g., youth services desk at (608) 873‑6284).

From: Eric Lindstrom <eric.lindstrom@stoughtonschools.org>
To: Rachel Lindstrom <rachel.lindstrom@stoughtonlibrary.org>
Date: 2025-01-18 07:42
Subject: Phone number you put on the draft budget doc

Hey Rach,

I was looking over the draft you left open on the dining room table last night—the one for the community foundation grant—and noticed you listed our home phone under “organization phone”: (608) 873‑9820.

Totally understand why you did it (you’re usually the one fielding questions anyway), but I wonder if it might be better to use something more official, like the library’s youth services line or even the city’s general information number. If this thing gets shared around, I’d rather not have random folks calling during dinner to ask what time garden nights start.

Given that my teaching contract at Stoughton High School got temporarily reduced to 60% for the 2023–2024 year after those budget cuts at the April 2023 board meeting, I’ve already picked up enough extra evening shifts at ACE Hardware. The last thing we need is the phone ringing off the hook when I finally get a night off. I know we’ve already put about $450 of our own money into seeds, tools, and lumber for those raised beds we tested in the backyard, but that doesn’t mean our personal number has to be the public contact forever.

Maybe just swap it to the library’s main line or your office number before you send it in? Happy to proofread again tonight once the kids are in bed.

Love,
Eric

metadata.title: Update contact info on garden materials

artifacts.content:
Subject: Signature & Date Needed on RoundUP Application Draft

Hi Rachel,

Thanks again for sending over the nearly-final draft of your RoundUP Community Micro-Grant application for the youth garden. I read through everything this morning, and the narrative looks strong.

A couple of small process items before we can move it to the city’s review queue:

1. The signature block on the last page is still blank. You’ll need to add your full name and the date you actually sign it. Since we’re targeting submission by **February 26, 2026**, I’d suggest you sign and date it no later than **02/25/2026** so we’re not scrambling at the last minute.

2. In the “Other Information” section, I appreciate your transparency about the minor trip-and-fall incident during the outdoor storytime back in September 2021 and the written warning from the City of Stoughton’s risk management office. That context about how you worked with James Keller and the insurance representative to tighten safety protocols is helpful.

3. I added a brief note acknowledging that some of the activities may initially serve kids and families who already use the library a lot, so the program might not reach many new or underserved residents right away. That’s not necessarily a deal-breaker, but reviewers like to see that you’re aware of it.

Once you’ve signed and dated the form, please scan the entire packet and email it back to me as a single PDF. If it’s easier, you can also drop off the signed hard copy at City Hall and I’ll have our clerk scan it.

Best,

Megan Schultz
Administrative Assistant
City of Stoughton – Community Services

metadata.subject: Signature & Date Needed on RoundUP Application Draft

artifacts.content:
RoundUP Application – Final Signature & Scan

Agenda:
- Print final version of RoundUP Community Micro-Grant application (latest draft from Megan)
- Carefully review budget table and narrative one more time
- Sign on applicant line and write in the date next to signature
- Double-check that the date matches within the packet (cover sheet, signature page, and internal routing form)
- Scan completed packet to PDF and email to Megan before end of day

Notes:
Megan wants the signed application in her inbox no later than **Thursday, February 26, 2026**, so she can route it to the city’s finance office on **02/27/2026**. I should probably plan to sign and date everything during this time block so I’m not trying to do it at the reference desk.

Remember to keep the language about how I’ve never actually managed a grant budget before—usually someone else at the library handles the money side—because the transparency about learning as I go on the tracking and reporting pieces seemed important to her.

Also, in the “Other Information” section, keep the explanation about how this isn’t a huge, formal nonprofit project, just me trying to get something going with the kids I see at the library and hoping a little seed money will help me figure it out as I go.

Bring:
- Flash drive (backup of scanned PDF)
- Sticky notes to flag any pages Megan might still want to tweak

Subject: Clarifying Signature Date vs. Project Start Date

Hi Rachel,

I’m reviewing the RoundUP application again before sending it to the city’s finance committee, and I noticed a small point of confusion around dates that we should clean up.

On the budget page you list the project start date as **June 1, 2025**, which makes sense given your plan to begin structured volunteer hours for Anna and her friend from Fox Prairie Elementary who is on juvenile probation after the October 2024 shoplifting incident at the Stoughton Walmart. However, on the internal routing form, you wrote **03/15/2026** as the date next to your signature.

That’s fine procedurally, but I want to be sure you understand that the signature date simply reflects when you signed the paperwork, not when the program activities begin. To avoid confusion, can you initial next to the 03/15/2026 date and confirm in an email that this is the correct date you signed? We don’t need you to re-date anything as long as the record is clear.

Also, your note about some activities primarily reaching kids and families who already come to the library regularly is helpful context. It shows you’re thinking realistically about who will benefit at first and how outreach might expand later.

If you can send a quick reply confirming that **March 15, 2026** is the actual date you signed the form, I’ll add this email to the application file and move it forward.

Thanks,

Megan

Megan Schultz
Administrative Assistant
City of Stoughton – Community Services

metadata.subject: Clarifying Signature Date vs. Project Start Date

artifacts.content:
Subject: Missing Date Next to Your Signature

Hi Rachel,

The RoundUP review panel met this afternoon, and overall your youth garden proposal was very well received. Before we can issue a formal award letter, though, the city attorney flagged a small technical issue on the signed application.

You signed the applicant line clearly, but you left the date line blank. For audit purposes, we need the date you actually signed the form. Since your cover email shows you scanned and submitted the packet on **January 29, 2026**, it would be ideal if you could:

1) Reply to this message confirming the date you signed the application (for example, “I signed the RoundUP application on 01/28/2026”), and
2) Bring the original hard copy to City Hall so we can have you handwrite the date next to your existing signature.

This doesn’t affect the substance of your proposal, including the section where you mention the minor trip-and-fall incident in September 2021 and the written warning from the City of Stoughton’s risk management office. The panel actually appreciated that you’ve used that experience to strengthen safety protocols for youth programs on city property.

Once we have a clear signature date on file, we’ll finalize the award documentation and send you the agreement to countersign.

Best,

James Keller
Library Director
Stoughton Public Library

metadata.subject: Missing Date Next to Your Signature

artifacts.content:
Sign & Date – Grant Agreement Countersignature

Description:
Time blocked to review, sign, and date the official RoundUP grant agreement that came from the city attorney’s office.

Checklist:
- Read through the full agreement again, especially the sections on reporting deadlines and use of funds.
- Confirm that the project period listed (06/01/2025 – 09/30/2026) matches what was in the original application.
- On the last page, sign on the line for “Project Lead / Applicant.”
- Write in the date directly under the signature line (target date: **04/10/2026** unless James asks for something different).
- Double-check that the date here matches the date in my email reply confirming when I signed.

Notes to self:
The agreement still references that this is a small, informal community project rather than a big formal nonprofit initiative. That’s accurate—it really is just me trying to get something going with the kids I see at the library and hoping a bit of seed money will help me figure it out as I go.

I also left in the candid note that I’ve never actually managed a grant budget before and that I’m learning the expense tracking and reporting pieces as I go. Slightly nerve-wracking, but honest.

Location:
Plan to do this at the library’s back office desk so I can scan and email the signed, dated agreement to Megan immediately afterward.

Subject: Quick question about signature date on RoundUP application

Hi Rachel,

I’m doing a final review of your Stoughton Utilities RoundUP grant application before we send everything over to their board, and I noticed that the signature line on page 4 is still blank.

Because you drafted most of this narrative back in late January, I just want to confirm what date you want to appear next to your signature. The packet currently references a tentative submission date of **February 28, 2026**, but if you end up signing earlier (say, February 20 or 21) we should make sure the date next to your name matches the actual day you sign.

While I’m in there, I also saw your note about your dad’s heart attack in January 2024 and the caregiving schedule you’ve taken on—driving to Mercyhealth Hospital in Janesville and then helping your mom with his medications on Tuesday and Thursday evenings. It’s a powerful detail and really helps explain why you’re trying to keep the youth garden sessions locked into that Wednesday 4:00–5:30 p.m. slot.

One small flag: the section about how funds will be used includes a line that says if there’s anything left over, you’d like to use the remainder to offer small gift cards as thank-you incentives so more kids actually show up and stay engaged. That might raise questions with the RoundUP committee, so think about whether you want to keep that in.

Can you email me back with the exact date you expect to sign so I can update the form before you print?

Best,

Carrie
Stoughton Public Library Foundation

metadata.subject: Quick question about signature date on RoundUP application

artifacts.content:
Title: Block time to sign & scan RoundUP grant forms

Description:

Goal: Make sure the RoundUP application packet is fully signed, dated, and scanned before Carrie’s internal deadline.

To do during this block:
- Re-read narrative sections about benefits to Stoughton community (including the part about Marjorie from the Senior Center and the other seniors who come to the garden after health setbacks).
- Double-check the paragraph where I mentioned that the project also benefits the Stoughton community by giving some of our more isolated seniors—like Marjorie, the 79-year-old widow who had a mild stroke in February 2022 at Stoughton Hospital—regular reasons to come outside and share stories with the kids.
- Verify that the “How Will the Funds Be Used” section doesn’t sound like I’m just trying to plug general gaps in the library’s youth programming budget this year, not just this specific project.
- Print final version of the application.
- Sign on all required lines and write the date clearly (aiming to sign no later than **February 27, 2026** so Carrie has wiggle room).
- Scan and email the completed packet back to Carrie before 4:30 p.m.

Reminder: After this, leave by 4:45 so I still have time to drive to Janesville to help Mom and Dad.

metadata.title: Block time to sign & scan RoundUP grant forms
metadata.location: Stoughton Public Library - Children’s Room Office

artifacts.content:
Subject: Re: Signature page timing for RoundUP proposal

Hi Carrie,

Thanks for catching the missing date on the signature line.

I just checked our family calendar and the week of the submission is already a little chaotic with Anna’s therapy follow-up and my Janesville trips, so I’d rather not leave the signing to the last second. I’m planning to come in early on **Friday, February 20, 2026** to print, sign, and date everything before the library opens.

That way, if the copier or scanner decides to misbehave, we still have time to troubleshoot before you send the packet on to Stoughton Utilities.

I hear you on the language about leftover funds. My intent was to be transparent—that if there’s anything left over after we buy supplies, I’d like to use the remainder to offer small gift cards as thank-you incentives so more kids actually show up and stay engaged—but I don’t want it to sound frivolous. Feel free to soften or trim that sentence if you think it will raise eyebrows.

I also appreciate your note about the personal sections. Including the piece about Anna’s hospitalization at UW Health’s American Family Children’s Hospital in May 2022 and her therapist, Dr. Holtz, recommending more outdoor time feels vulnerable, but it’s a big part of why I believe in this project.

Let me know if you need me to initial any changes before I sign on the 20th.

Best,
Rachel

metadata.subject: Re: Signature page timing for RoundUP proposal

artifacts.content:
Title: Sign RoundUP application in front of notary

Description:

Meet with City Hall notary to finalize signature page for Stoughton Utilities RoundUP grant application.

Plan:
- Leave library at 2:40 p.m. and walk over to City Hall.
- Bring printed application packet, driver’s license, and the latest edits Carrie emailed.
- Review the “Benefits to the Stoughton Community” section one last time, including the paragraph about how the garden strengthens community resilience for families like Mom and Dad in Janesville who have been quietly helping Lisa cover part of her medical school loan payments since her husband was laid off from the GM supplier plant in March 2024.
- Confirm that the line about how, to be honest, the main benefit for me is getting to test out some program ideas I’ve been wanting to try—even if they don’t draw a big crowd right away—still sounds okay.
- Sign in blue ink on all required lines while the notary watches.
- Write in today’s date clearly next to my signature (target: **March 5, 2026**, as long as nothing delays us).

Afterward, drop the notarized packet back at the library and ask Carrie to scan and submit before the end of the day.

metadata.title: Sign RoundUP application in front of notary

artifacts.content:
Subject: Draft language for certification & signature section

Hi Rachel,

As promised, here’s a proposed revision for the certification paragraph that appears directly above your signature line on the RoundUP application. I’ve tried to keep it in your voice while tightening a few spots.

I’ve also added a parenthetical note reminding you to fill in the date when you sign. We’ll aim for **March 1, 2026** as the official signing date, since that still gives us a cushion before the utilities board packet deadline.

---

“I certify that the information provided in this application is true and complete to the best of my knowledge. I understand that RoundUP funds must be used for the purposes described herein, including supplies for the youth garden project and related programming at the Stoughton Public Library. (Signature of Applicant) ____________________________   (Date) ____________.”

---

You can handwrite the date when you sign; no need to retype the form.

I left in your brief references to caregiving for your dad after his heart attack at Mercyhealth in Janesville, Anna’s time at UW Health’s American Family Children’s Hospital, and the student referred by Ms. Hovland at Fox Prairie who started counseling at Stoughton Counseling Services after those playground altercations in March 2023. They paint a compelling picture of why you’re so invested in this work.

Let me know if you want to tweak anything before we finalize the certification language and print.

Best,

Carrie

metadata.subject: Draft language for certification & signature section
